text:
RAD File / Dossier de la SAR : TB9-11169
Private Proceeding / Huis clos
Reasons and decision ? Motifs et décision
Person who is the subject of the appeal
XXXX XXXX
Personne en cause
Appeal considered / heard at
Montreal, QC
Appel instruit / entendu à
Date of decision
September 4, 2020
Date de la décision
Panel
Patricia O'Connor
Tribunal
Counsel for the person who is the subject of the appeal
Lorne Waldman
Conseil de la personne en cause
Designated representative
N/A
Représentant(e) désigné(e)
Counsel for the Minister
N/A
Conseil du ministre
REASONS FOR DECISION
OVERVIEW
[1] XXXX XXXX (the Appellant) is a citizen of Ukraine. She appeals a decision of the Refugee Protection Division (RPD) rendered on April 10, 2019, denying her claim for refugee protection.
[2] The Appellant submits that the RPD erred by rejecting her claim on the basis that her evidence lacked credibility.
[3] After considering the evidence, I allow the appeal, finding that the RPD erred in its assessment of the claim and that the Appellant credibly established that she is a Convention refugee whose experience of cumulative discrimination leads to a serious possibility of persecution as a result of her mixed Ukrainian/Roma ethnicity in Ukraine.
BACKGROUND
[4] According to her Basis of Claim (BOC), the Appellant was born in XXXX 1938, into a mixed family-her father was Roma and her mother was Ukrainian. They lived is a small village in Ivano-Frankivsk with a strong Romani population. After the outbreak of World War II, they left the region but the Romani who remained in the village were killed by the Nazis along with Jewish people.
[5] After the war, the Appellant's family was reunited and returned to live in the village of XXXX. The Appellant describes that the anti-Roma sentiment lessened after the war.
[6] The Appellant obtained her XXXX XXXX and got married. The Appellant and her husband settled in a village in the region of Lviv where there were many Roma and mixed Roma-Ukrainian families. They were both XXXX who XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. Her husband died in 2013.
[7] Over the years, the Appellant states that she experienced some discrimination from Ukrainian villagers who perceived her as Roma. With the collapse of the Soviet Union in the early 1990s, nationalist tendencies strengthened in Ukraine and the public attitude towards Roma deteriorated.
[8] The Appellant did not hide her Roma ethnicity and took part in holidays and celebrations.
[9] In 1992, a new XXXX who held nationalist anti-Roma views, was hired XXXX XXXX XXXX where the Appellant was a XXXX. In 1993, the XXXX informed the Appellant that Ukrainian villagers complained that a Roma was XXXX XXXX XXXX and the Appellant was forced to resign. XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX she believed that education is a means for the Romani to escape poverty and hopelessness. The villagers resented that she helped so many Roma children.
[10] In 2013, the Appellant was grieving the loss of her husband. At the same time, political unrest began in the country and anti-Roma harassment and persecution increased in Ukraine. From 2014 to 2016, the Appellant, who is perceived as Roma, noticed a marked deterioration in the way she was treated by fellow Ukrainians. While the anti-Roma sentiment directed at her remained ongoing, she continued to teach Roma children.
[11] Meanwhile, some Roma families who were displaced as a result of the conflict in East Ukraine, relocated to the Appellant's village where their presence was resented by other villagers. When she tried to help the newcomers, she was also met with open hostility by Ukrainians.
[12] In August 2016, anti-Roma sentiment worsened when a Roma man murdered a Ukrainian girl in Odessa. In December 2016, threatening and insulting graffiti including swastikas and slogans such as "death to Roma" and "Roma live here" appeared on the door of her home and that of other Roma families. She was openly insulted by other Ukrainian villagers. Other Roma families said they could not count on the police for assistance as they were nationalistic and prejudiced towards Roma.
[13] In February 2017, the Appellant went to see a local police officer to request protection from the ongoing harassment. The officer was rude and refused to open a complaint. He said that she should relocate to a Roma settlement if she did not like living in the village.
[14] Upon hearing of her plight, the Appellant's son invited her to Canada, and she received a visitor visa in XXXX 2017. She was not feeling well and was reluctant to leave her husband's grave. She decided to stay longer in Ukraine until she felt stronger.
[15] On XXXX XXXX, 2017, she was at home with Roma friends celebrating her birthday when someone threw a rock through her window, shattering the glass. The person left behind a note stating: "Roma, get out of Ukraine". The police officer would not open a file and dismissed the complaint as a joke.
[16] In June 2017, a wealthy Ukrainian named XXXX XXXX wanted to develop land in the village. A real estate agent approached the Appellant and other Roma families to purchase their land on behalf of the XXXX family. The Appellant declined as she was not interested. Moreover, she learned that when another Roma family refused a low offer to purchase their land, the agent threatened that "the village would be cleansed of Roma one way or the other" because powerful people were interested in the land. Only Roma and mixed Roma families were approached for the sale of their home which led the Appellant to suspect that the businessman (who is known to be a staunch nationalist) wanted to get rid of the remaining Roma in the village.
[17] In August 2017, the home of a Roma family in the village was set on fire while they were out of town. This family had previously refused to sell their home to the agent. The police did not investigate the incident which the Appellant assumes is connected to the construction of XXXX summer home.
[18] In September 2017, the agent became enraged when the Appellant refused his final offer to sell her property. He warned that she would regret the decision greatly.
[19] On October 8, 2017, she was returning from visiting her husband's grave when she was accosted by a Ukrainian man who yelled: "How long will you Roma poison our native Ukraine?" and he proceeded to strike her in the face causing her to fall to the ground. He then continued to kick her in the legs. Passersby called an ambulance and she was diagnosed with XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX requiring hospitalization for one week.
[20] The police officer who was called to the hospital appeared uninterested. Instead, he asked the Appellant why she did not move somewhere else. The police closed the file in November 2017.
[21] Following her discharge, she received threatening telephone calls telling her to leave the village before it was too late.
[22] In December 2017, she went to stay with her sister in another village with a Roma community. On XXXX XXXX, 2018, she was feeling better and travelled to Canada where she made a refugee claim on March 22, 2018.
[23] The RPD dismissed the claim, finding overriding concerns with the credibility of the evidence. The RPD found that the Appellant's testimony was evasive and largely a recitation of her BOC narrative. Moreover, the RPD considered aspects of the story implausible and inconsistent. Her delay leaving Ukraine was found to undermine the credibility of her subjective fear. Documentary evidence aimed at corroborating the claim was given low probative value.
DETERMINATIVE ISSUE
[24] The Appellant raises several issues on appeal. I find that the determinative questions are the following:
- Did the RPD erred in its assessment of the Appellant's credibility.
- Does the Appellant's cumulative experience of discrimination, harassment and violence in Ukraine lead to a serious possibility of persecution?
SCOPE OF THE APPEAL
[25] The Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) reviews decisions on a standard of correctness after conducting an independent assessment of the evidence before it.1 Deference may be required when the RPD enjoys a meaningful advantage in the assessment of the credibility of oral evidence.2 I have applied the correctness standard in the present case.
ADMISSIBILITY OF EVIDENCE ON APPEAL
[26] The Appellant requests to submit the following documentation as new evidence on appeal:
Item 1: Press release from Minority Rights Group International, May 8, 2019, announcing the publication of a new report on Roma in Ukraine presented in Kyiv on May 8, 2019.
Item 2: A report from Minority Rights Group Europe, May 8, 2019 "Roma in Ukraine - A Time for Action: Priorities and Pathways for an Effective Integration Policy".
Item 3: A report "Ukraine: A year after attack on Roma camp in Kyiv, no justice for victims", Amnesty International, April 16, 2019.
[27] As a result of the disruption caused by COVID-19, the Appellant was given a further opportunity to provide supplementary information. On July 14, 2020, Counsel submitted the following additional documents relating to the treatment of Roma in Ukraine:
Item 4: Carlo, Andrea, "We need to talk about the rising wave of anti-Roma attacks in Europe" The Independent, 28 July 2019.
Item 5: "Freedom in the World, Ukraine Country Report 2020", Freedom in the World.
Item 6: Human Rights in Ukraine. Website of the Kharkiv Human Rights Protection Group. "Criminal investigation initiated after Mayor of Ivano-Frankivsk orders forced eviction of Roma, 23 April 2020.
Item 7: Matache, M., Leaning, J., and Bhabha, J. "The shameful resurgence of violent scapegoating in a time of crisis." Open Democracy, 5 May 2020.
Item 8: Deychakiwsky, Orest. "Freedom in Ukraine: Moving in the right direction?" The Ukrainian Weekly, 10 July 2020.
[28] Guided by s. 110(4) of IRPA, I note that all of these reports which concern the situation of the Roma in Ukraine, post-date the rejection of the claim by the RPD (April 10, 2019). I find that the reports are objective information from credible and reliable sources and is relevant to the assessment of the Appellant's forward-facing risk in Ukraine. As such, Items 1 through 8 are admissible as new evidence on appeal.
ORAL HEARING
[29] I have admitted new evidence on appeal. However, this evidence does not raise a serious issue with respect to the Appellant's credibility which would justify allowing or rejecting the appeal. Rather, this evidence is just one of several factors leading me to grant the present appeal. As such, I must proceed without an oral hearing in the present case.3
ANALYSIS
Credibility
The RPD erred by failing to make clear findings of fact about the Appellant's ethnic identity
[30] On appeal, Counsel argues that there is an overriding problem with the analysis conducted by the RPD: while the Panel member appears to implicitly accept the Appellant's ethnicity, the Panel member makes other peripheral findings designed to impugn the credibility of the Appellant's ethnic identity, without notifying her of the issue or giving her a chance to respond.
[31] I agree.
[32] For instance, when asked at the RPD hearing which ethnicity appears on her birth certificate, the Appellant expressed uncertainty, stating that she did not look at the document which is back in Ukraine. She added that her birth certificate probably states that her ethnicity is Ukrainian (like her mother), as opposed to her father's Roma ethnicity.
[33] The RPD found it astonishing that the Appellant never looked at her birth certificate and did not know what ethnicity was written on it. The RPD found that the Appellant provided an evasive answer which impugns her credibility.
[34] In other words, it appears that the RPD is impugning the credibility of the Appellant's ethnic identity because she responded to questions about her birth certificate in a way that was deemed evasive and it is entirely implausible that she would not have looked at her birth certificate and remembered which ethnicity is indicated.
[35] With respect, this finding is problematic on two fronts.
[36] First, evasive means that she answered questions in a way that is not straightforward or clear; and that she avoided providing a response to the question.4 Yet, the Appellant did, in fact, respond to the question about the ethnicity that is indicated on her birth certificate by expressing uncertainty. She testified that it was probably her mother's Ukrainian ethnicity which appears on her birth certificate, but she was not sure. I fail to see why the expression of uncertainty in this context would impugn her credibility.
[37] The Appellant was never asked why her father's Roma ethnicity is probably not indicated on her birth certificate and why her mother's Ukrainian ethnicity is probably mentioned. There are different reasons that could explain why the Appellant's Roma ethnicity is not indicated on her birth certificate. However, in the absence of clear evidence and mindful of the historical context in which she was born (1938) and her evidence that Roma from her village were persecuted and murdered like Jewish people during the Second World War, I find it was an error to impugn her credibility simply because her Roma ethnicity is probably not indicated on her birth certificate.
[38] Secondly, the RPD found it astonishing that the Appellant did not look at her USSR-issued birth certificate back in Ukraine. This was found to further impugn her credibility. While, the RPD took a literal approach to the Appellant's response, it appears that she was trying to explain that her birth certificate is not a document that she looks at with any regularity. I find it was an error to impugn her credibility based on this response since it is entirely possible that she relies on other types of identity documents in Ukraine instead of her USSR-issued birth certificate.
[39] It is true that the Appellant testified that she did not look at her birth certificate which remains in Ukraine, I fail to see why this response detracts from her assertion that she is of mixed ethnicity.
[40] The RPD also gave little weight to a letter written by XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX in Toronto5 attesting to her Roma ancestry along with photographs showing the Appellant at Roma activities there. The RPD found the letter is skeletal in nature and the person was not present at the hearing to provide further explanation.
[41] I agree with Counsel that impugning the Appellant's credibility on this basis is in error.
[42] First of all, I have reviewed the document which includes a membership certificate giving the Appellant access to events and services provided by the Toronto Roma Community Centre. The XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX indicates that "after interviewing this individual at great length" he certifies that she is a Roma-identified individual and belongs to the XXXX Roma clan of the tribal group specifically located in the former Ukraine.
[43] The RPD hearing is an inquisitorial process. If the Panel member had questions about the content of the letter, he certainly could have requested that the author of the letter be called to testify. I note that there was a discussion at the outset of the hearing regarding potential witnesses and the Panel member suggested that the Appellant's son could be an important witness. The XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX was not raised by the Panel member.
[44] Most significantly, while the RPD gives "low probative weight" to this letter, it remains entirely unclear if he disbelieves that the Appellant is of mixed Ukrainian/Roma ethnicity.
[45] In sum, I find that the adverse credibility findings reached by the RPD with respect to the birth certificate and letter from the Roma community leader, are unsustainable. The RPD also erred by failing to make clear findings of fact on a critical issue underpinning the claim: the Appellant's ethnic identity.
Impugning the Appellant's credibility based on her tendency to recite lines from her Basis of claim narrative
[46] The RPD states that the main reason for impugning the Appellant's credibility is because she was unable to provide any detail beyond what is written in her narrative when asked to explain the basis of her allegations. The RPD found that she simply recited the lines from her written statement which is "a very strong indication that the claimant's narrative was not a recounting of her experiences but rather a fabrication of which she had no knowledge beyond the words of the narrative."6
[47] I agree with Counsel that this finding is unsustainable.
[48] First of all, I note that the Appellant (a woman in her eighties) provided a very detailed narrative, describing her lifelong experience as a person of mixed Ukrainian-Roma ethnicity in Ukraine. For example, her BOC narrative describes the experience of her family during the Second World War; their experience in the aftermath of the war; and her experience as a person of mixed ethnicity living in a small village in Ukraine where she was XXXX XXXX XXXX and assisted Roma children. Since she provided a very detailed narrative, it remains entirely unclear why her credibility is being impugned for testifying in a manner which is consistent with her narrative.
[49] The RPD gives two examples to support the assertion that the narrative is a fabrication.
[50] First, the RPD notes that when she was asked why she thought the home of her Roma neighbours was set on fire, she simply responded that the homeowners are Roma and no one likes Roma. She did not expand on this testimony.
[51] With respect, I cannot agree with the RPD. My review of the evidence points to the fact that the Appellant actually stated that she "assumes" that the neighbour's house was set on fire because they are Roma and a wealthy Ukrainian man with nationalist views wanted to purchase the property. It is difficult to fault the Appellant for failing to provide a more detailed response when she testified about the fire since she explained that she was making "assumptions" about why the fire was set.
[52] Likewise, when asked to explain what she reported to the police about the October 2017 assault, the RPD found that the Appellant simply repeated her narrative, describing "what happened", even after the RPD repeated that he was not asking "what happened" but rather what information she had provided to the police about the incident. To be frank, I fail to understand the issue here. The police were called to the hospital after the Appellant was physically assaulted and treated for her injuries. Does it strain credibility that the Appellant would report the incident itself to the police?
[53] In sum, I find that the RPD erred by impugning the Appellant's credibility on the basis that her testimony was a recitation of her BOC narrative. This finding is unsustainable and will be set aside.
Medical treatment
[54] The Appellant testified that she was hospitalized and received medical treatment in Ukraine after she was assaulted in October 2017. When asked if she received other medical care, she said she was bedridden for two weeks when she arrived in Canada. Noting that the medical report mentions extensive injuries, the RPD found that the absence of further medical care impugns the credibility of the medical report which includes specific recommendations for future care.7
[55] I agree with Counsel that the RPD has erred in its approach to the medical report.
[56] First of all, the excerpt from the XXXX XXXX XXXX Hospital medical chart indicates that the Appellant was in the hospital from XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, 2017.8
[57] She was noted to have XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. She had a XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, and she was treated for her injuries. Upon discharge, it was recommended that she be followed by a doctor for two months; that she takes prescribed medication for one month and that she be referred to an XXXX.
[58] The RPD found that the Appellant had "extensive injuries" and that the absence of further medical treatment following her discharge impugns the credibility of the medical report. However, this finding is not supported by the facts. To the contrary, the Appellant's XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX at the hospital where she was also treated for the injuries that she sustained during the assault. She left the hospital when she was ready for discharge. Upon discharge, she was prescribed medication. She was not asked at the RPD hearing if she ever took the prescribed medication. While it was recommended that she be followed by a doctor for two months after her discharge, I find that the Appellant reasonably explained that she could not obtain medical follow-up as she went to stay with her sister and was not registered at her sister's address and therefore not eligible for medical insurance.
Delay leaving Ukraine
[59] The RPD found that the Appellant's credibility is further impugned because she waited 10 months to leave Ukraine after receiving her visa to Canada. The RPD found that her explanation that she was not feeling well enough to travel to Canada "raises questions" considering her testimony that she was unable to get out of bed for two weeks after arriving in Canada but yet reports no hospitalization during the period that she remained in Ukraine and even left her village to go and stay with her sister.
[60] Here, the RPD missed pivotal evidence. The Appellant reasonably explained that she delayed leaving Ukraine as she was afraid to leave her house unattended after her neighbour's house was set on fire and the authorities did not investigate. She also testified that she further delayed leaving Ukraine "permanently" as she felt deep sorrow leaving her husband's grave. While the RPD also found that her assertion that she was not well enough to travel raises questions because she did not report being hospitalized during the period that she remained in Ukraine, I do not agree that one must be hospitalized to establish that you are unwell.
[61] All things considered, the Appellant has reasonably explained her delay leaving Ukraine.
The Appellant's lack of interest in her home in Ukraine
[62] The RPD found the Appellant's credibility further undermined and her allegations are fabricated as she did not make any inquiries about her house since arriving in Canada.
[63] I agree with Counsel that nothing turns on this issue. The RPD has impugned the Appellant's credibility based on the assumption that she has an ongoing interest in her house. To the contrary, the Appellant testified that she was not concerned or interested in her home as her main priority is to remain safe with her adult children in Canada, where she hopes to live on a permanent basis.
[64] Up to this point in my analysis, I find that the RPD made overriding errors in the assessment of the Appellant's credibility.
[65] I turn now to correct the error by conducting my own assessment of the claim.
[66] On a balance of probabilities, I find that the Appellant provided cogent and credible evidence establishing that she is of mixed Roma-Ukrainian ethnicity and that she has experienced ongoing incidents of discrimination, violence and harassment based on her Roma ethnicity. Her allegations are corroborated by a letter from the Roma community attesting to the fact that she is from the XXXX Roma clan. Moreover, a medical report documents her hospitalization and treatment for injuries following a physical attack, and a police letter shows her efforts to obtain assistance following the assault was unsuccessful. She fears that she will be subject to further incidents of discrimination, harassment or violence if she returns to Ukraine based on her Roma ethnicity.
The Appellant's cumulative experience of discrimination, harassment and violence in Ukraine leads to a serious possibility of persecution
[67] A well-founded fear of persecution refers to a "sustained or systemic violation of basic human rights."9 A high threshold is required to demonstrate that incidents of discrimination or harassment amounts to persecution. Case law explains that the incidents must be "serious or systematic"10 such as repetitive or persistent infliction of acts of cruelty, injury or annoyance.11
[68] The United Nations High Commissioner for Refugee (UNHCR) Handbook states that in some situations, various elements taken together may produce an effect on the mind of the claimant that can reasonably justify a claim to a well-founded fear of persecution on cumulative grounds. While it is difficult to lay down a general rule as to what cumulative reasons can give rise to a valid claim to refugee status, this will depend on all of the circumstances which include looking at the impact of the discrimination on the person.12
[69] The UNHCR Handbook provides further guidance in the present case:
54. Differences in the treatment of various groups do indeed exist to a greater or lesser extent in many societies. Persons who receive less favorable treatment as a result of such differences are not necessarily victims of persecution. It is only in certain circumstances that discrimination will amount to persecution. This would be so if measures of discrimination lead to consequences of a substantially prejudicial nature for the person concerned, e.g. serious restrictions on his right to earn his livelihood, his right to practice his religion, or his access to normally available educational facilities.
55. Where measures of discrimination are, in themselves, not of a serious character, they may nevertheless give rise to a reasonable fear of persecution if they produce, in the mind of the person concerned, a feeling of apprehension and insecurity as regards his future existence. Whether or not such measures of discrimination in themselves amount to persecution must be determined in the light of all the circumstances. A claim to fear of persecution will of course be stronger where a person has been the victim of a number of discriminatory measures of this type and where there is thus a cumulative element involved.13
[70] In Bayrak,14 the Federal Court emphasized that in assessing whether sporadic discrimination amounts to persecution, it is important to consider the effects on the person. The Federal Court also noted that some mistreatment can be considered persecution on the basis of its seriousness and that the definition of "discrimination" is inadequate to cover conduct that included acts of violence and death threats.15
[71] In assessing whether the Appellant's treatment rises to the level of being serious and systematic, I have conducted an individualized, fact-based assessment of her claim which includes an analysis of the Appellant's personal circumstances.
[72] The Appellant is an elderly woman in her eighties who lives alone. Contrary to many Roma, she has a birth certificate; she was raised outside a Roma settlement and obtained XXXX XXXX XXXX. This being said, she experienced lifelong discrimination and harassment on account of her Roma ethnicity. Her first experience of mistreatment based on her Roma ethnicity goes back to World War II when her family managed to flee Nazi persecution while those who stayed behind were murdered. After the war, her family reunited. She eventually got married and worked XXXX XXXX XXXX for many years. She did not conceal her Roma ethnicity and participated in different events with her Roma friends. Notwithstanding her mixed ethnicity, she is perceived as Roma by others.
[73] In 1993, she was forced to leave her job XXXX XXXX XXXX when Ukrainian families complained that a Roma XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. This prompted her to teach Roma children from her home for which she experienced a steady stream of harassment and discrimination.
[74] However, the situation markedly worsened since the rise of nationalism in Ukraine in 2014. She describes that her home was vandalized. There were anti-Roma slogans and threats. In November 2017, she was the subject of an anti-Roma motivated assault which required hospitalization. Her life has been threatened. Romani and mixed Romani families were threatened and pressured to sell their homes so that a nationalist businessman could develop the land. While she describes staying in the village to remain close to her husband's grave, the escalating incidents caused her to feel apprehension and insecurity, prompting her to flee her home and stay with her sister in Ukraine until she came to Canada.
[75] On the whole, I find that the cumulative acts of discrimination, harassment and violence constitute persecution. The dangers and the risk that she faces in Ukraine is compounded by the weakness and vulnerability that comes with advanced age.16 The fact that she proudly supports Roma families and does not conceal her Roma ethnicity points to the fact that she has a higher profile and is at greater risk of future attacks from those members in her community who object to Roma.
[76] The Appellant's prospective risk is supported by the available objective evidence17 which reveals that the Roma are a minority ethnic group in Ukraine representing 0.6% of the population. The vast majority of Roma and other minority groups are integrated within the general population and suffer little discrimination although one source notes that this is because they frequently hide their ethnic background by self-identifying as Greeks, Azeris or Armenians.18 Since 2018, developments in the eastern Ukraine have contributed to a rise in xenophobic incidents.19 For instance, there was an increase in violent attacks on Roma camps and hate crimes but the police did not always intervene to protect them.20 In a recent report, it was found that the leading ethnic minority attracting negative bias in Ukraine is the Roma with a percentage of 40.67%.21 New evidence provided by Counsel on appeal, continues to point to the upsurge of anti-Roma attacks in Europe which often goes unchecked, as far-right populism is gaining momentum (Items 4 and 5). A news article from April 2020 (Item 6) describes that a criminal investigation was initiated after the Mayor of Ivano-Frankivsk in Ukraine ordered the forced eviction of Roma.
[77] While it is believed that crimes directed at the Roma are underreported, the majority of reported cases of hate crimes against the Roma are characterized by extreme cruelty and scale.22
[78] In July 2018, the UN Special Rapporteurs to Ukraine issued a press release condemning heinous acts of intimidation and violence against the Roma minority in Ukraine. The press release states:
Ukraine must take immediate action to stop what amounts to a "systematic persecution" of the country's Roma minority, who have been targeted in a series of violent attacks.... We are also seriously concerned at the growing hatred and racially-motivated violence against this community-and in particular against its most vulnerable members, women and children...." 23
[79] "Women from minority ethnic groups such as Roma are at particular risk of domestic abuse and street violence at the hands of non-Roma."24
The Appellant cannot benefit from adequate state protection or an internal flight alternative (IFA) in Ukraine
[80] In the present case, the RPD did not address the issue of state protection or IFA given its finding that the Appellant's evidence is not credible. Having determined that the RPD erred in its assessment, I will continue the analysis of the claim.
[81] A state is presumed to be capable of protecting their citizens, except when there is evidence that the state is in a complete situation of breakdown. To rebut this presumption, a refugee claimant must "adduce relevant, reliable and convincing evidence which satisfies the trier of fact on a balance of probabilities that state protection is inadequate".25
[82] A contextual approach is required when assessing whether a refugee claimant has rebutted the presumption of state protection.26 The Gender Guidelines also provide guidance when assessing the adequacy of state protection with respect to gender-related claims.
[83] I have reviewed documentary evidence regarding Ukraine's ability to offer protection to the Roma people.
[84] In 2013, the Government put into place a Strategy for the Protection and Integration of the Roma Ethnic Minority in Ukraine which runs up to 2020. However, this Strategy has been criticized for having no budget and for having been mainly unimplemented.
[85] In 2018, Human rights groups expressed concern about the "increasingly organized set of nationalist hate groups committing violent attacks on ethnic minorities (especially Roma).... The HRMMU (Human Rights Monitoring Mission to Ukraine) noted that the failure of police and prosecutors to prevent these acts of violence, properly classify them as hate crimes, and effectively investigate and prosecute them created an environment of impunity and lack of justice for victims. A June 13, 2018, joint open letter to Ukrainian authorities from Human Rights Watch, Freedom House, Amnesty International, and Frontline Defenders also expressed concerns about the spike in attacks and impunity, and noted, "the inadequate response from the authorities sends a message that such acts are tolerated."27
[86] Human Rights Watch reported that in most cases, police failed to respond or effectively investigate.28
[87] A press release from the UN Special Rapporteurs to Ukraine in July 2018, deplored the absence of effective measures to protect members of the Roma minority and expressed concern about an alleged climate of impunity and lack of accountability with no prompt, thorough and impartial investigation of criminal acts directed at the Roma people.29
[88] Freedom House reports that a 2012 law introduced a nonexclusive list of grounds on which discrimination is prohibited. However, "these protections are inconsistently enforced, and the Romany minority ... generally only receive police protection or justice for attacks against them when there is intense pressure from civil society or international observers."30
[89] In the present case, the Appellant has also provided evidence about her lived experience seeking assistance from the police in Ukraine. However, she was met with inaction by the police who instead recommended that she move away from her home and community. On the whole, I find that the Appellant has provided clear and convincing evidence to rebut the presumption of adequate state protection.
[90] With respect to the issue of IFA, the onus is on the Appellant to demonstrate that it is unreasonable for her to seek refuge in a different part of her country before resorting to international protection.31 In the present case, I accept that the discrimination experienced by the Roma in Ukraine is the same everywhere in Ukraine. As such, I do not find the Appellant can avail herself of an IFA.
CONCLUSION
[91] Pursuant to ss. 111 (1)(b) of the IRPA, I allow the appeal, finding that the Appellant is a Convention refugee.
(signed)
Patricia O'Connor
Patricia O'Connor
2020-09-04
Date
PO/lp
1 Canada (Citizenship and Immigration) v. Huruglica, 2016 FCA 93 (CanLII), paras 78, 103.
2 Ibid., paras 58, 59 and 70.
3 See ss. 110 (3) and 110 (6) of IRPA.
4 Cambridge Dictionary, Subverbo "evasive", dictionary.cambridge.org.
5 Exhibit RPD-1, RPD Record, Letter from the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX p 114.
6 Exhibit RPD-1, RPD Record, RPD decision, para 9.
7 Ibid, para 10.
8 Exhibit RPD-1, RPD Record, Excerpt from the Appellant's medical chart, XXXX XXXX XXXX Hospital, p 106.
9 Canada (Attorney General) v Ward. [1993] 2 SCR 689, 723.
10 Sagharichi v. M.E.I. [1993] F.C.J. No. 796, 182 N.R. 398, 42 A.C.W.S. (3d) 494, para 3.
11 Rajudeen v. M.E.I. [1984] F.C.J. No. 601, 55 N.R. 129, para 14.
12 Bayrak v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) 2013 FC 1056.
13 Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, Handbook and Guidelines on Procedures and Criteria for Determining Refugee Status under the 1951 Convention and the 1967 Protocol relating to the Status of Refugees, Reissued, Geneva, December 2011, paras 54-55.
14 Bayrak, Supra.
15 Ibid, para 19.
16 Ibid, para 18.

However, in accordance with the IRB Policy on National Documentation Packages in Refugee Determination Proceedings, 5 June 2019, s. 5 (https://irb-cisr.gc.ca/en/legal-policy/policies/Pages/national-documentation-packages.aspx), my forward-facing assessment of the claim is also informed by updated information regarding the country conditions in Ukraine found in the current NDP for Ukraine, 30 June 2020 and the new evidence provided by the Appellant on appeal.
